speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape two. Today's date is May 31, 1995. This is an interview with Livia Bitton-Jackson. I was asking you how your family survived during the period of the Hungarian occupation without their store and their livelihood.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, actually, it's an excellent question. In the beginning, they-- the Hungarian authorities did not take away, did not confiscate the merchandise. The store had a lot of merchandise. The stockroom was full of all kinds of merchandise. And the war broke out, and much of this merchandise was rare.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the beginning, my parents managed to sell quietly to neighbors. They would come to our home, and my parents would sell merchandise to them. All kinds of things. We had dried goods, and cotton, wool, for instance, for sewing. And feed for the animals.

speaker: interviewee
text: They would usually come late at night. And the neighbors were very good to us. We were close friends with them. And they would-- many of them would come to buy from us also to help us out. Or, perhaps, because it was cheaper than in the store. My father would sell them things at the lower price. Because for us, this was the only livelihood. And then, if I recall, just about a year later, the authorities came and put a seal on the store. And they said, you cannot touch the-- the merchandise. They put a seal on our stock room. And then shortly afterwards, the military police carted off everything that-- that we had, completely emptied the store and stock room out. And of course, this source of income was finished. My mother had a very-- was very handy with her fingers. And she started to sew. She started to sew privately for, again, neighbors, friends. Other customers who never knew was before found out about it. She was sewing dresses, dresses for the children. Again, remember, in that age and in that town, there was no such thing as a store-bought dress, no such thing. Everything was handmade. And this was it. Dressmakers made your clothes. Even men's shirts were not ready-bought. My mother made men's shirts, slacks. People would go to a tailor to have a jacket and a coat made. But the slacks and shirts were made by dressmakers. So this is what my mother was doing. She was sewing. And very early, she taught me how to finish off the inner seams and also how to do hems. And I started to work with her late at night after I did my homework, sometimes quite late at night. And quite early, I started to have back aches from this, sitting and finishing off. You know, sitting in this position. And-- and I was very tall for my age. And so I imagine, also, the fact that I had, like, a long torso, sitting in a position where I was sewing caused a lot of back-- back discomfort already then. This was one source of income. Another one was my father, even though we had a store, he was really not a businessman. And he was not really tending the store most of the time. He was a Hebrew scholar. He was-- almost always, I remember him with a large folio of the Talmud or of the Bible and studying all the time. And even in the store, he would sit there in the corner. While my mother would be chatting with the customers or taking care of them, my father would sit and study.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was offered a position in the town school, the Hebrew school, to teach the children. Hebrew school, in our place, didn't mean A-B-C, or the Hebrew alphabet, or just teaching the children to read. It meant really teaching them higher learning, the-- the commentaries on the Bible or the commentaries on the Talmud.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so rather than bringing someone in, a teacher from another town, my father was then given a position. And he was-- he was teaching. A very small salary he received. And by the way, he hated it. He was not-- he didn't love teaching. And I can't understand. Because I love teaching. But he didn't like it. He preferred to study than-- than to teach. But he was doing it. He was teaching. And-- and this is how we more or less managed till-- till we were taken away into the ghetto, and then into camps. As a matter of fact, my mother was saying later on that she wishes that my father didn't have to teach, because he hated it so much in those last few years while we were together. This added to his unhappiness. And we had enough resources. My parents were middle class people. And they had enough financial resources to manage even without that. But my-- my mother and my father probably were worried, not knowing how long it's going to take and how much resources will be used up. So it-- they want to supplement us with my father's teaching and my mother sewing.

speaker: interviewer
text: During that time and even before the Hungarian occupation, how much did your family know about Hitler and Germany and what was going on in the rest of Europe at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, being very near the border of-- of Czechoslovakia occupied, you see, we were quite-- actually, I must correct what I said earlier, that the entire Slovakia was handed over to Hungary. Actually, this didn't happen. It's more correct that a strip of land alongside the Danube, the northern bank of the Danube, became Hungary. Another part, two-thirds, actually, remained Slovakia, independent, under the leadership of Tiso, who, by the way, was the Archbishop of Slovakia. And he was an ally of Hitler. And there, too, was a Nazi regime right across the border much more severe than Hungary. So that deportations to Auschwitz and other camps began already in 1942. And between 1941 and 1942, the Jews of Slovakia, which was about 2, 3 kilometers, the border from us-- our town was practically sitting on that Slovak border. And so right across the border, the Jews were being deported to Auschwitz in 1942 and, already in 1941, taken to all kinds of internment camps and concentration camps in Slovakia, itself. They were dragged away from their homes. And some people escaped across the border during the night. So we knew what is going on. We knew that the Jews are being dragged away from their homes during the night. They disappear. They're never seen again. Some people were shot on sight on the street. Some people who resisted deportation were-- were killed. So this was quite early. And from then, we heard about Auschwitz. As early as 1942, we already heard. Even the name Auschwitz, we knew. And we knew about things that are happening in Poland. Because some people escaped from Poland to Czechoslovakia or to this area in Hungary. And there, the local community was hiding them. I remember someone who came at one point, perhaps as early as 1941. I was only 10 years old then. But I remember that he came, and he spread a horrible rumor. And everybody said the man is insane for making up such a story. And the story that he was spreading was that the Jews in Poland, also right across the border from the eastern part of Czechoslovakia, where rounded up outside the town. And they were given shovels to dig their graves. They had to dig ditches. And the whole family had to stand at the edge of the ditch, and they were shot. And they fell into the ditch. And the other members of the town had to cover them with the Earth until their turn came to be shot and buried in the grave that they had to first dig themselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all these things were true. This is what was happening. But nobody wanted to believe it. But you know what? I believed it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was only 10 years old, and I believed it. And I knew this must be true. And I knew in my guts that nobody would make up stories like that. And I think that the grownups believed it, too. It is just something in the human psyche that doesn't want to believe the horrible, the-- the unknown, the thing that looms ahead. And rather than believe it and be petrified, you dismiss it and you say, no, this cannot be true. And whoever says it is making up stories. So we knew these things. I also remember, and this was just about the same time, that some of our neighbors where listening to the radio and the windows were open. And you could hear every day-- because we had no radios anymore. You could hear the radio blasting. And some-- we had already gradually, gradually, some neighbors who exhibited as anti-Semitism openly. And so some of them would leave their windows open when Hitler was speaking. And Hitler was taking every day. And the Hungarian radio would put on Hitler's rabble-rousing speeches. And he would shout in German. And many of us spoke German. And I spoke German from childhood on. And there was this recurring theme he would yell. We are going to play football with the heads of Jews. We'll cut their heads off and play football with it. And to me, this was so terrifying. I couldn't sleep, because I'd visualize bloody heads of the local people in my town rolled on the ground. And we had a large football field in-- in Samorin. And I visualized them playing football with the heads of Jews. And it was terrifying. And when I told my father about it, I was crying, and I couldn't sleep. And he said, oh, don't take that literally. This is just Hitler wanting to raise the anger and hostility against Jews. And it's only figurative speech. It's not literal. But we knew. You-- you didn't have to be a prophet to realize that these things were going on around us and that we are heading for doom. The only thing that would have saved us is an early Allied victory over Germany. And unfortunately, Hungary was occupied by Germany quite late. And when I say unfortunately, I meant to say that. Unfortunately, 1944 when already the Allies landed in Western Europe, the Americans landed in Western Europe, that was the time when we were deported to Auschwitz. So, the Allied victory didn't save us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had your family made any inquiries into being able to leave the area prior to the Hungarian occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, which I didn't know. It's-- it's not even puzzling to me. Because, you see, by the time the deportation reached us, it was so well-organized. It was systematic. It was quick. People were immediately registered Jews and rounded up. And within three weeks, we already were gone from our hometown into a ghetto in another town, under strict military police supervision. There was no time to escape. Also, you see, where we live is a flat area. It's-- it's-- it's called the Small Danube Plain, plain, flat. In the hills of Slovakia, many Jews escaped into the hills and were hiding out in the forests. We didn't have forests. We had only agricultural land, all flat, flat as far as the eye can see. There was no place to hide. And our neighbors couldn't hide us, because the supervision and the military occupation was so thorough. There was-- there was no way to run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had our parents thought about, years before, perhaps they could have done something. I don't know what, maybe escape. But where?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, Slovakia, which was next door, was already occupied by the Nazis in 1938. When we were attached to Hungary, Slovakia was taken over by a Nazi regime. And so there was really no place to run.

speaker: interviewer
text: I believe you mentioned that earlier, in the '30s, your father had looked into going to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. That was quite early. As a matter of fact, we were registered at the American embassy as early as 1935. Because the year before, my father's brother went to America. And when Hitler rose to power in 1933, everything changed in Europe. The rumor spread very quickly that Jews are in danger. And my uncle sent papers to my father. You needed an affidavit and all kinds of papers to ensure that he's going to undertake our support, and we won't be a burden on the American government, et cetera. These were the things that you had to prepare. And with that, he went to Prague, to the American embassy, to register us. But you know, America had very strict immigration laws. And one of the stipulations was that everybody had to register on what was called a quota, a list. And you put your name on a list. And according to the number of people on the list, you have a chance to be admitted or not admitted. A certain number of people were admitted from Czechoslovakia each year. I don't know how many. Whether it was in the thousands, or only in the hundreds, I don't know. But once that number was admitted, then you had to wait for the next year's opening. And if the list was very long, it took years. So my father was told that the quota, is very-- the list is very long, and he has to wait his turn.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in 1938, before the Hungarians came, we already knew rumors that we'll be occupied by Hungary, and we'll be cut off from Czechoslovakia. My father went to Prague and-- and submitted a special request that we are going to be cut off from Czechoslovakia. And we are in danger.

speaker: interviewee
text: Perhaps, we could get some kind of a-- a-- preferential treatment. But the American embassy told us, no, you have to wait your turn. And my father came back. Later that year, in September, we were cut off from Czechoslovakia. And from that point on, we were no longer on the Czechoslovak quota, because now we belong to Hungary. And we could not put on names on the Hungarian quota from the American Embassy, because we were not considered Hungarian citizens. We were Jewish aliens from this point on. My father was not a Hungarian citizen. Some people who were born in Hungary could apply for Hungarian citizenship. But then again, very soon the Jew law came out. And all Jews, even in Hungary, lost their citizenship, which meant that we were not entitled to be registered on the American quota. So we were trapped. We were really, really trapped and ready for Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you going to school during the Hungarian occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We-- we went to school until the German occupation. The Germans came to Hungary unexpectedly on March 19, 1944. German troops marched into Budapest. And during the course of that week, all schools were closed, Saturday, I remember, the end of that week. If I remember correctly, March 19 was a Sunday. And on Saturday, the end of the week, our teacher came in. By that time, I went to what here would be called a junior high. There it was called middle school, to middle school, which was not the Jewish school anymore. So we went to school also on Saturday. And the teacher came in, the homeroom teacher, and announced in the morning that class-- pack your things, go home, school is closed. And she did not explain why or what. And she said, we are going to be out of school. She didn't know how long. But she didn't even give us a chance to ask questions. She just left the classroom. And we're standing there in shock. And we went home. And all the schools in Hungary were closed on that day, under the order of the occupying German forces. Even though Hungary was a friendly nation, but it wasn't handled in very friendly fashion by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And perhaps, just so we'll know, you could tell us your family's names and ages at the time the German occupation. So we're talking about March, 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes-- 4. OK. I just turned 13. My birthday is February 28. My brother just turned, not-- he was 17 and 1/2.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His birthday is in October. His name is Armin Friedmann. My name was Elvira Friedmann. My middle name was Olivia, but they called me Ellie for short. And I couldn't stand the name Elvira, and I still don't. And so they-- everybody agreed not to call me that. So the called me Ellie. And my father was born in 1897. So let's do a quick arithmetic. '44, he was 47. My mother was 2 and 1/2 years older than my father, which is quite unusual. She was 49 and 1/2, sort of, at this point.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her first name was Laura. Laura. Her maiden name was Roth. R-O-T-H. And Laura Friedmann.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell us about what happened once the Germans occupied Samorin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for me, it was very dramatic. Because my brother was, at this point, a student in Budapest. Budapest is the capital of Hungary. And he went to Budapest to study at the Jewish Teachers Seminary the September before. In 1943, September, he went out to Budapest. And we saw him only very rarely, because the distance was quite great. It took about half a day of train travel. Trains probably weren't so great then. And so we didn't really see him. I don't know, probably for the-- for the four holidays, four Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, he was home. But then he left. And I probably didn't see him since then. March 19 in the morning, the Germans occupied Budapest. Tanks, German tanks, rolled into Budapest. Nobody knew. We didn't know anything. But during the night, my brother arrived. I was sleeping, and I heard voices in the kitchen. And I thought it another raid by the military police. And whenever the military police came on a raid, my mother was always very worried about me. I was a very young girl. But still, she wasn't sure whether they would rape a young girl or not. Violence was beginning. And so she always told me, whenever you hear sounds during the night, quickly cover up-- up to your neck and be sleeping. Don't open your eyes. And when the military police comes into the bedroom, put the cover over your head, and don't let them see you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is what I did. I heard some sounds. I quickly covered my whole body and waited and waited and waited, and nobody came into the bedroom.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the conversation is going on in the kitchen. So against my mother's strict orders, I got out of bed, and I tiptoed to the kitchen door. And the kitchen door was closed, but we had a glass window over it with a curtain that you could see through. And I see my brother talking to my parents. So I came into the kitchen. I was very excited. I was crazy about my older brother. And I hugged him. And we called him bubbe. That's a German nickname, like in English you would say sonny. Because [NON-ENGLISH] is, in Germany, a little boy. And so, bubbe is like sonny.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, bubbe, what are you doing here? So, very quickly, I understood that he came home because he saw-- as he was going to school, he saw German tanks marched into-- march-- marching through the main highway in Budapest, the main boulevard which he had to take a streetcar on in order to go to school. So he must have been a very smart guy, very smart boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because nobody else did this. There were quite a few other young people from Samorin who studied in Budapest. Nobody came home.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the only one. Instead of going to school, he turned around, went straight to the train station, took the train home and arrived in the middle of the night. And my parents said, why did you come?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and, you know, it cannot be true. Because we don't know anything about it. Well, to make a long story short, the next morning my father went to the synagogue, because that was where news reached you. Nothing, nobody knew about the occupation. Noontime, nothing, nobody, newspapers, radio, nothing. So they told my brother, my father and my mother, look, you must have made a mistake. Maybe it was a demonstration with tanks. But I saw the Nazi flags. I saw the swastika, huge swastika. I saw the swastika on the building of the parliament. Because this is where he was going to school with the street car. And they said, maybe it was just a demonstration of solidarity with the Germans. You must have made a mistake. There's no point in wasting time. Go back, you're-- you're wasting school. For my parents, everything was learning, school. You know, God forbid, you should lose a day of school. So 2 PM, there was to train to Budapest. They put him on the train, and he went back.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the evening, a neighbor of ours, a lawyer whose son was studying in Budapest, rushed over and said, did you hear? The Germans occupied Budapest. And they're arresting Jews on sight. Everybody, all the Jews there are being arrested. Children, students from schools are being arrested and put on trains and disappear. And do you know anything about it? Did you hear anything from bubbe? And then my father admitted to him that bubbe was home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SOBBING] And my mother broke down. I never saw my mother cry, never. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It just right that I can stop. OK.
